speaker: interviewer
text: Nesselroth. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ms. Nesselroth, you had started talking about the going to the munitions factory from Skarzysko-Kamienna. Was the town already made into a ghetto that-- at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was in the beginning of 1940, when they established the ghetto. But they could not close-- it was the-- the-- Skarzysko was in the middle between the ammunition factory and the railroads tracks, the railroad station. And that way they-- they could not close. But later they make it smaller ghetto. And then they close it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why could they not close the town off?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- because the people who came to-- from different religious, they have to go through the ghetto. And when they make it smaller, they put the-- the people back from the-- the opening, when they used to go through. They took away this part. They let them go that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember when they first-- when the-- it first became a ghetto and opened--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you-- where were you when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in working with Dr. Cahan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the day when you first heard or saw that happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We saw that happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first day, what they closed the ghetto, they came and they confiscated the-- the Jewish high schools, the Jewish universities, the-- the theater was closed. The Institution of Learning was closed. The-- the whole thing about the Jews, and they ask us to wear this [NON-ENGLISH], the band of shame, they said. [NON-ENGLISH], with David's-- David's Star. And-- and that time we-- I-- I-- and those-- one of mine friend, we went, and my brother, we went to-- out of the ghetto, to our friend's. And we left their fur coat for my mother's.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we'd go out. And we go-- coming back from there, we didn't have our coats already. And a Polish policeman with a German soldier, I didn't know at that time what he was, a soldier, that he was a-- I didn't know at that time, what he was. Well, he stopped us. And he said, what are you doing? You're Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said no, I'm not Jewish. And I saw my brother. And I said to him, go away from here. And he didn't look like a Jewish boy. Go home and tell them what happened, you see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in one moment, an old lady came out from the forest, holding you know, she used to go for this-- the-- piece of-- of wood. You know? And she said-- when I said oh, well, I'm not Jewish. I'm not Jewish. Of course, I'm Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- he-- the-- the German didn't understand what I was saying, in Polish. And he ask him. Well, he didn't understand German either, the Polish-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] policeman. And this lady said, no, they are Catholics. They are Catholics. Let them go. Let them go. Like somebody would send her from the-- somewhere. And she disappeared. And-- and he let us go. This was the-- the thing in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: So later on we had to make up a-- a ID. Then also we'd go to this-- Gestapo. And there it was the-- the German. And it was the-- the Polish policeman. And he-- The same ones you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He said, what are you do here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, nothing. I said, what, nothing? You are here in the-- this building. I said, yeah, I'm here in this building. You remember? I said, what? I saw you. I said, me? I never go out from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he again, and the same thing. And I said, no, you didn't see me. And he let me go. You know, this. It was very hard, the ghetto. The ghetto was so hard that the-- the-- it was very crowded. Because the people from [INAUDIBLE], the part of-- of Poland who-- well, in-- included in the German-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] German government, they were-- the Jews and the Poles were evacuated from there, sending back to the General-- they call it General-- Government General, this part of Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and it was so crowded because to-- for example, to one room sometimes was 10 people. You know? Sickness. And everything you can find there. Sickness and filth and dirt and everything. People were dying every day. Where was your family--

speaker: interviewee
text: In--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and you living?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are--

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- our house was included to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anyone else living with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my uncle and my aunt and her two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they happen to come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --evacuated. And they came to us. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family talk about the ghetto, or what was going on? What do you remember of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They said only be a human being. Be a human being. Don't watch the others, what they're doing. It's not you. Don't watch them. Be a human being. And the history was so terrible that when I came to the-- to the ammunition factory, I saw what he said, was nothing. Was nothing. The history was so terrible that he lost his will to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are you talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father. Because he was taking, also--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Later-- later he was taking-- well, he wanted to go with me. He wanted to go, to be with us. But because of my sister, my mother said no, I'm not going. I'm going with my daughter to the evacuation. And I used to go home all the time from work, after 12 hours of working, about two hours to walk home. And I-- I came home. At five o'clock in the morning I have to get up and to go to work again. And so I saw the whole tragedy while there. I saw my grandmother was shot in front of us. She was a nurse in a-- and a wife, what they call children-- a wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: A midwife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: A midwife, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: A midwife for the poor, for the poor. As she was shot. She was 86 years old. How you can forget those things?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you mind telling us--

speaker: interviewee
text: How you can forget those things. And my uncle who lives in-- together with us has two daughters. He had three daughters. Two daughters, age nine and seven, were taken away from him. My aunt was taken to the-- another ammunition factory. The-- he was-- they want him to-- he was a young man. They want him to go with them, to work for them. He said no, I'm not going. I'm going with my children. And he died in Treblinka. Now, this-- from our city-- from other city, they-- they were-- all of them went to Treblinka. The city where my husband lived, Wadowice, where the Pope was born together, in the same city, where my husband was born. They were good friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there it went. So it was closer to Auschwitz. They all perished at Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: In our city, in Kielce, Radom, and the city closer to the west-- to the east went to Treblinka. And others, it was-- thousand of others, camps. Smaller and bigger at ours camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back, if you don't mind, to the time when you were-- when Skarzysko-Kamienna was turned into a ghetto. At that time, when it was first a ghetto but an open ghetto, where was it that you were working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the ghetto was open still open?

speaker: interviewer
text: While it was still open.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working by Dr. Cahan. One day I was walking home. The-- the Germans-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] --caught me. And they ask me to go in to clean the offices there. So I went there. And when I clean up one floor, they brought dirty water and they pour on the floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was asking him what he is doing. He beat me up so terribly that I was black and-- and blue. And I came home and my parents, what happened? And I said, I fell. On your face? I said, yeah. They didn't say anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: It hurts to this day. It hurts that-- nobody can understand those things. Well, the ghetto was terrible. You see, I work at the ammunition factory. I go home. But in the ammunition factory I have a girlfriend who went to school with me. And she tried to help me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: She bring me a sandwich to eat, you know? I ask her to go to our friends, what we left, some things to go and pick up a salad and bring me a bread. Only bread. You didn't need any more. And she would go and to do that. That's the reason we survive a little bit. And then the underground in Warsaw sended out money for the people to rest a little. And they thought that we're going to be able to have a-- a uprising there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean, send money to rest?

speaker: interviewee
text: For us.

speaker: interviewer
text: To rest?

speaker: interviewee
text: To rest, because we were completely out of our-- physically, mentally, we are nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- and how--

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- when you don't eat, you don't remember your name.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the money? How were you supposed to use the money?

speaker: interviewee
text: $500 for-- for our family. We-- we received $500.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that would help you rest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, helped me to-- to give some money for-- at the factory, where I worked there, to the people who work with me. But they were free. Free-- they were not free. They had to have a special card with stamps every day, the hour. [INAUDIBLE], the [INAUDIBLE], had to write down his initials, that there. If they don't have it, they were arrested.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you use the money?

speaker: interviewee
text: I gave those people the money to bring me bread, to buy bread for that. You know? Bread, at that time cost $35-- 35 zloties of bread. That was not too much money. And there was four-- and other people, we always share with them in the barracks, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you-- when you talk about the barracks, when did you-- I thought you were living at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they-- they-- this whole 200 people go home every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, or your-- you don't mean you lived in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- so you bought the money-- you-- you used the bread--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The money to buy bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do with the bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: We ate some. And some we give to the barracks there. Because there were people already locked up in the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say we, who do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, my father, [INAUDIBLE]. And so we did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your job for a while was in the health department, you said, as a dental assistant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that come to and end? How did that change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when they took me to the ammunition factory I lose my job.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what--

speaker: interviewee
text: He couldn't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job, and what kind of work did you do in the ammunitions factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: I work in the ammunitions-- in the-- on the-- factory's office, where the SS took away the wages from our 1,500 people, from mine-- mine division, where I work. And I-- I have also to keep-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to keep the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to keep the-- the-- know when you-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cannot remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: To keep the books, you know? In order to see if this is enough or this we have to order or that, things like that. With me, it was a [GERMAN]. You know what a [GERMAN] is? A lady who her ancestor were Germans, and she pretended that she is a German too. She was terrible. In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was-- she had her own desk. And I have my own desk. And she always ask me to do her work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: She had also, those kind, to write down and to-- not special. Not-- not anything special. And she used to bring for herself a-- a sandwich with coffee, for example. She would have left coffee. She would pour in the sink in front me. She would finish her work. She would-- tied her chess-- her chair to the desk. If I want to sit down, I cannot sit down because it's tied to the thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, you had no chair?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a chair. But when I want to see-- see what she has done with her work, so I cannot see anything. So anyway, one time I brought-- I had-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a-- like a coat-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] --with stripes there. And you know, the Germans are very particular.

speaker: interviewee
text: Clean, they want a nice office and something. So when I came in, she said, you're not going to put this in the closet. I said, why? Because you have lice. We had lice, it's true. I said, OK. I put it out. So then Herring came in. He said, what's here? I said, she asked me to put that out. Oh, she [GERMAN]. You know? What do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- to her, that she is-- she is a terrible person. She's not a German. She is what he said she is. She is something what-- she never been a German. She's not even a-- a human being. He told me that she's not.

speaker: interviewee
text: So OK. So another day she did the same thing. I put it in. He came in, same thing. You put it in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I put it in.

speaker: interviewer
text: In where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the closet. And she again, take it out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So after a while, he ask me if I can do her work. I said all the time I did her work. She ask me to do her work when he was on the German lunch. I did it for her, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I brought with me some handkerchief. I was crazy about handkerchief. And I left that and I couldn't have it on me. I left in the drawer of my desk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, you don't need those anymore. The Germans are going to kill you. I said, how do you know? I know. I know everything. I said, you know such a thing and Herring didn't tell me? That something is going to happen, but you? I said, I've got-- so when Herring came and I said, I heard that we are going to be-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --liquidated or we're going to be killed. Who told you? Is it true? He said, no, it's not true. It's not true. I said-- and he ask me, who told you? I said, I-- I am not going to tell you. Because you're going to kill this person. I'm not going to tell you. I'm not the informer. No. So the next time she-- we have a few Polish people who were on the underground. And they hate her, you cannot imagine. And I told her what she did, that she doesn't let me do this and that and that. So they came in. They put some nails under the-- the chair with the-- they put some gasoline or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had some ulcers later, terrible ulcers from it. And she was sick. When she was sick he-- he fired her. And I took over the whole thing. And one day I was talking with somebody. He used to take this person to the office and beat her up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Herring.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: The sadist.

speaker: interviewer
text: And whom did he take?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't-- I could not talk to anybody. Because he would take it in front of me to beat her up. One time I have a aquarium there. I don't have aquarium in my house. I would never have it. And fish died. You know, fish died so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose aquarium was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aquarium with-- with fish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who did it belong to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To him, what I took care of it. And the fish died. And I receive-- oh. He beat me. You cannot imagine. And I was the good one. I know everything, what to do. And I was-- when something was wrong in the books, he said, no, it's not her fault. It must be your fault. You have wrong. But he beat me up. This was a sadist. This was something-- you cannot imagine what this was. Another human being completely. And we work with that thing like that. We cannot-- The second one was [? Frau-- ?] first name was Dora Fawlowski.

speaker: interviewer
text: The second what? You said the second one?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lady. The second one was also a lady. A lady, she was-- what do you call? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She used to take, you know, the people, take a iron brush and take the chemicals that you washed the-- the-- the-- the ammunition, you wash in a special solution. They're so terrible, bad, that when you work only with it, the fumes makes you sick, makes you have the whole hand sick with rash, everything. And they-- she used to wash them, take them naked, in front of everyone, wash those people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- our-- our inmates, they washed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say our inmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish people were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you-- you talking about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Men, men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who worked there, or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: They worked there at the ammunition factory, with ammunition, with everything, they worked there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the people who were in the-- in the barracks already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did-- what was this lady's job?

speaker: interviewee
text: She used to come to control everything, go around. One was having a prayer book with him. He was-- during the-- our lunch hour, during he was praying. And she took him out. And they shot them. He was praying to God against the German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him doing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. All of us were there. Everyone has to go out, every time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Every time we have something happen, we used to go and watch them. One time it was about five people. The Germans pretended that it was sabotage. They hanged them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they-- you know? And 20,000 people went out, watching them. And they make us to watch them, how they dangled on this thing. And when somebody cannot watch anymore, they used to go with the pages, with the whip, whip them. You know? And they stood there like they are the-- the first to go to heaven for that. And they stood there. And I said, God, where are you? Why do you punishing every one of us? We didn't do anything against anybody. What are you doing? Your heart is breaking, out-- piece by piece.

speaker: interviewee
text: During the ghetto, they-- I told you before, during the ghetto, when my sister was taken away, a-- across the-- our-- our-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] [SIGHS] --apartment, where we lived, across the apartment, she just gave birth to a-- a little girl. And they came into-- to the house. They took the baby out. They kicked the baby like this is a-- a football. And I still remember her scream. And her-- to this day, sometimes we sit at night, when I cannot sleep, I saw her. And I saw the baby. And her staying there in the-- in the-- in the doors, and the screaming, terribly screams. And there are those good people. They are those people who taught the children that Hitler was not a bad man. He was the best man. They are those people who have-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --the way to promote those Nazis. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And help those to escape the punishment, what they really receive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh God, help me. [SIGHING]
